title: Right to Life
summary: Use of lethal force by state agents must be no more than absolutely necessary in defence of any person from unlawful violence, to effect lawful arrest, or to quell riot. Planning and control of operations must minimise risk to life.
source: ECHR Article 2; McCann and Others v United Kingdom (1995), App. no. 18984/91
subject: officer

title: Prohibition of Torture
summary: Torture is absolutely prohibited — no derogation permitted even in war or public emergency. The threshold for 'torture' (as opposed to inhuman treatment) is severity of suffering inflicted intentionally for purpose of obtaining information or confession.
source: ECHR Article 3; Selmouni v France (1999), App. no. 25803/94 (Grand Chamber)
subject: officer

title: Inhuman or Degrading Treatment
summary: Even a single slap by police to a person under their control violates Article 3 — any recourse to physical force against a person deprived of liberty which has not been made strictly necessary by their own conduct diminishes human dignity. Threat of torture also constitutes inhuman treatment.
source: ECHR Article 3; Bouyid v Belgium (2015), App. no. 23380/09 (Grand Chamber)
subject: officer

title: Conditions of Detention
summary: Detention conditions must respect human dignity. Overcrowding, lack of medical care, denial of food/water/sanitation can amount to degrading treatment regardless of officer intent.
source: ECHR Article 3; Kudła v Poland (2000), App. no. 30210/96
subject: officer

title: Procedural Duty to Investigate Ill-Treatment
summary: When credible allegation of ill-treatment by police is made, state must conduct effective, independent, prompt investigation. Failure to investigate is a separate Article 3 violation.
source: ECHR Article 3 (procedural limb); Assenov v Bulgaria (1998), App. no. 24760/94
subject: officer

title: Right to Liberty — Lawful Arrest
summary: Arrest is lawful only on reasonable suspicion of having committed an offence. 'Reasonable suspicion' requires facts or information satisfying an objective observer.
source: ECHR Article 5(1)(c); Fox, Campbell and Hartley v UK (1990), App. no. 12244/86
subject: officer

title: Right to Be Informed of Arrest Reasons
summary: Everyone arrested must be informed promptly, in a language they understand, of the reasons for arrest and any charge. Information must be sufficient to challenge lawfulness.
source: ECHR Article 5(2); Conka v Belgium (2002), App. no. 51564/99
subject: officer

title: Prompt Production Before a Judge
summary: Arrested person must be brought promptly before a judge. 'Prompt' generally means within 48-96 hours; longer requires exceptional justification.
source: ECHR Article 5(3); Brogan v UK (1988), App. no. 11209/84
subject: officer

title: Right to a Fair Trial — Pre-Trial Phase
summary: Fair trial protections extend to pre-trial police interrogation. Statements obtained in violation of Article 6 cannot ground a conviction.
source: ECHR Article 6(1); Salduz v Turkey (2008), App. no. 36391/02 (Grand Chamber)
subject: officer

title: Right to Silence and Against Self-Incrimination
summary: Suspects have the right to remain silent and not contribute to incriminating themselves. Adverse inference from silence is restricted; coercion to speak violates Article 6.
source: ECHR Article 6(1); Funke v France (1993); Saunders v UK (1996)
subject: officer

title: Right of Access to a Lawyer
summary: Access to a lawyer must be granted from the first police interrogation. Statements made without lawyer present, absent compelling reasons, are presumptively inadmissible.
source: ECHR Article 6(3)(c); Salduz v Turkey (2008), App. no. 36391/02 (Grand Chamber); EU Directive 2013/48/EU
subject: officer

title: Right to an Interpreter
summary: A suspect who does not understand the language used must be provided with free interpretation during police interrogation and proceedings. Translation of essential documents is also required.
source: ECHR Article 6(3)(e); EU Directive 2010/64/EU on the right to interpretation
subject: officer

title: Right to Respect for Private Life
summary: Police interference with private life (searches, surveillance, retention of biometric data) must be lawful, pursue a legitimate aim, and be necessary and proportionate.
source: ECHR Article 8; S. and Marper v UK (2008), App. no. 30562/04 (Grand Chamber)
subject: officer

title: Bodycam Recording — Data Protection
summary: Body-worn camera recordings constitute personal data processing. Must have legal basis, clear retention policy, subject access rights, and proportionality in deployment. Continuous recording without notice may violate Article 8.
source: ECHR Article 8; GDPR Article 6 and Law Enforcement Directive (EU) 2016/680
subject: officer

title: Freedom of Expression — Criticism of Police
summary: Verbal criticism, insults, or protest against police — even when offensive — is generally protected speech. Arrest as retaliation for protected expression violates Article 10.
source: ECHR Article 10; Janowski v Poland (1999), App. no. 25716/94
subject: officer

title: Freedom of Peaceful Assembly
summary: Peaceful protesters may not be dispersed or arrested merely for participating in an unauthorised assembly. State must show tolerance toward peaceful gatherings; force is last resort.
source: ECHR Article 11; Kudrevičius v Lithuania (2015), App. no. 37553/05 (Grand Chamber)
subject: officer

title: Prohibition of Discrimination
summary: Police treatment must not discriminate on grounds of race, ethnicity, religion, gender. Ethnic profiling in stops and searches without objective justification violates Article 14 read with Article 8.
source: ECHR Article 14; Timishev v Russia (2005), App. no. 55762/00
subject: officer

title: Non-Refoulement
summary: States may not expel, deport, or return any person to a country where there are substantial grounds to believe they face a real risk of torture or inhuman treatment. Applies absolutely, including at maritime borders.
source: ECHR Article 3; Saadi v Italy (2008), App. no. 37201/06 (Grand Chamber); Hirsi Jamaa v Italy (2012)
subject: officer

title: Human Dignity — Inviolable
summary: Human dignity is inviolable and must be respected and protected. Treats this as foundational principle informing interpretation of all other rights — especially relevant when subject is restrained, vulnerable, or in custody.
source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 1
subject: officer

title: Prohibition of Torture (Charter)
summary: EU institutional restatement of the ECHR Article 3 prohibition. Binds EU bodies and Member States when implementing EU law.
source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 4 (mirrors ECHR Art. 3)
subject: officer

title: Right to Liberty and Security (Charter)
summary: Mirrors ECHR Article 5: no arbitrary arrest, lawful procedure required, prompt information of reasons, prompt judicial review.
source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 6
subject: officer

title: UN Convention Against Torture — Definition
summary: Defines torture as severe pain or suffering, physical or mental, intentionally inflicted by or with consent of public official, for purpose of obtaining information, punishment, intimidation, or discrimination.
source: UN Convention Against Torture (1984), Article 1
subject: officer

title: Proportionality of Police Use of Force
summary: Force used must be strictly proportionate to threat. De-escalation required where feasible. Firearms only when strictly unavoidable for protection of life.
source: ECHR Articles 2 and 3; UN Basic Principles on the Use of Force and Firearms (1990); Council of Europe Recommendation Rec(2001)10
subject: officer

title: Officer Identification Requirement
summary: Police officers performing duties must be identifiable — by name, badge, or service number — to enable accountability. Removal or concealment of identification during force is a procedural failing.
source: Council of Europe European Code of Police Ethics (2001), Article 45; ECHR Article 3 procedural limb (identification enables accountability)
subject: officer

title: Duty of De-escalation
summary: Law enforcement officials shall, as far as possible, apply non-violent means before resorting to force. Communication, distance, time, and negotiation are required where feasible.
source: UN Basic Principles on the Use of Force (1990), Principles 4–5; Council of Europe Code of Police Ethics, Articles 35–37
subject: officer

title: Criminal Threats Against Public Official
summary: Serious threats of violence against police officers performing official duties are criminal offences across EU jurisdictions, with aggravation when officer is on duty.
source: Most EU Member State penal codes (e.g., Italian Codice Penale Art. 612; German StGB §§ 113, 241; French Code Pénal Art. 433-3)
subject: citizen

title: Assault on a Public Official
summary: Physical violence against a police officer on duty is criminalised across EU jurisdictions. Includes spitting, striking, and any physical interference with arrest.
source: Italian Codice Penale Art. 336–337 (resistenza/violenza a pubblico ufficiale); German StGB § 113; French Code Pénal Art. 433-6
subject: citizen

title: Resistance to Public Official
summary: Active physical resistance — distinguished from passive non-compliance or verbal protest — to a public official performing duties is a criminal offence. Verbal protest alone is protected expression.
source: Italian Codice Penale Art. 337; analogous statutes across EU Member States
subject: citizen

title: Flight from Lawful Stop
summary: Failure to stop upon lawful police signal, particularly in a motor vehicle, is a criminal offence aggravated when reckless.
source: Member State traffic and criminal codes (e.g., Italian Codice della Strada Art. 192; German StGB § 142 analogues)
subject: citizen

title: Obstruction of Investigation
summary: Destroying evidence, lying to investigators about identity, or aiding a suspect's escape constitutes obstruction across EU jurisdictions.
source: Member State penal codes (e.g., Italian Codice Penale Art. 378 favoreggiamento; German StGB § 258)
subject: citizen
